ضيف هي
Terms in this set (41)
How much of the total body weight of the average adult is muscle tissue
40-50%
The different types of muscle tissue differ from each other by
All of the above
Which of the following is NOT a major function of muscle tissue
Storing energy
This is the property of muscle that gives it the ability to stretch without damage
Extensibility
In an isometric contraction the muscle develops tension but does not
Shorten
This is a band of connective tissue that surrounds muscles
Epimysium
When connective tissue extends as a broad flat layer, the tendon is referred to as
Aponeurosis
For every nerve that penetrates a skeletal muscle there are generally how many arteries and veins
One artery and one or two veins
Axon terminal clusters at the ends of neuromuscular junctions are referred to as
Synaptic end bulbs
After the fusion of myoblasts, the muscle fiber loses its ability to do what
Go through mitosis
The sequence that muscle action potentials must go through to excite a muscle cell
Axon of neuron, sarcolemma, T tubules
The mitochondria in muscle fiber are arranged
Closest to the sarcolemma
These are the contractile organelles of the muscle fiber
Myofibrils
This part of the skeletal muscle cell releases calcium when stimulated by the T tubules
Terminal cisterns of sarcoplasmic reticulum
The sarcoplasmic reticulum is used for storing
Ca+
Which of the following contain thin filaments
I band AND A band
Which of the following contain thick filament
All of the above
Myofibrils contain
All of the above
Which of the following functions as a motor protein in all three types of muscle tissue
Myosin
What regulatory proteins can be found on an actin molecule
Tropomyosin and Troponin
Titin is found in a sarcomere
From M line to Z disc
Which of the following is used to reinforce the sarcolemma
Dystrophin
In the sliding filament mechanism, the thin filament is being pulled towards the
M line
The sarcoplasmic reticulum releases calcium ions into the cytosol
At the beginning of a contraction
What energizes the myosin head
ATP hydrolysis reaction
What is needed for the contraction cycle to continue
All of the above
This results from a muscle action potential propagating along the sarcolemma and into the T tubules
Excitation
The signal to excite a muscle cell must cross the neuromuscular junction by the diffusion of acetylcholine across the
Synaptic cleft
How many molecules of acetylcholine need to bind to open the ion channel of the ACh receptor
More than 1000
How do muscles produce ATP
All of the above
Creatine phosphate and ATP together create enough energy for a muscle to contract for
15 seconds
This consists of a somatic motor neuron plus all the skeletal muscle fibers it stimulates
Motor unit
This is a brief contradiction of all muscle fibers in a motor unit in response to a single action potential
Twitch contraction
This is also referred to as the period of lost excitability
Refractory period
A sustained contraction is
Tetanus
Increasing the number of active motor units is called
Motor unit recruitment
This is the least powerful type of muscle fiber
Slow oxidative fiber
Which of the below structures if found in cardiac muscle tissue but not skeletal muscle tissue
Intercalated discs
Which of the following exhibits autorhythmicity
Cardiac muscle fibers
Smooth muscle tone is due to the prolonged presence of what in the cytosol
Calcium ions
Hyperplasia is
An increase in the number of muscle fibers

    id: "q-joint-43",
    questionText: "Explain why the shoulder joint is both more mobile and weaker than the hip joint.",
    answerText: "The shoulder joint has a shallow socket (the glenoid fossa) into which the head of the humerus fits, and the labrum only slightly deepens the socket. The head of the humerus is larger in relation to the socket. It is deepened by a number of ligaments, but because of its structure, tends to be weaker than the hip joint. It is highly movable and can rotate easily. The hip joint has a deeper socket (acetabulum) into which the head of the femur inserts and is also strengthened by ligaments. Because of the angle at which the femur fits into its socket, along with the depth of the socket, the hip is a stronger, less mobile joint, made to support the weight of the body.",

I have added a toggle button to the Quiz results view that allows users to show or hide explanations. This setting persists across sessions.

    *   Imported `Eye` and `EyeOff` icons.

    questionText: "Explain why the shoulder joint is both more mobile and weaker than the hip joint.",
    answerText: "The shoulder joint has a shallow socket (the glenoid fossa) into which the head of the humerus fits, and the labrum only slightly deepens the socket. The head of the humerus is larger in relation to the socket. It is deepened by a number of ligaments, but because of its structure, tends to be weaker than the hip joint. It is highly movable and can rotate easily. The hip joint has a deeper socket (acetabulum) into which the head of the femur inserts and is also strengthened by ligaments.     id: "q-joint-43",
    questionText: "Explain why the shoulder joint is both more mobile and weaker than the hip joint.",
    answerText: "The shoulder joint has a shallow socket (the glenoid fossa) into which the head of the humerus fits, and the labrum only slightly deepens the socket. The head of the humerus is larger in relation to the socket. It is deepened by a number of ligaments, but because of its structure, tends to be weaker than the hip joint. It is highly movable and can rotate easily. The hip joint has a deeper socket (acetabulum) into which the head of the femur inserts and is also strengthened by ligaments. Because of the angle at which the femur fits into its socket, along with the depth of the socket, the hip is a stronger, less mobile joint, made to support the weight of the body.",

**Extracting HTML Data**

I'm currently focused on parsing the provided HTML snippet. My plan is to extract the questions, answer options, and correct answers. I intend to structure this data for direct integration into the `questions` array within the `data.ts` file. I'm aiming for a straightforward, programmatic approach.


**Parsing HTML Elements**

I've successfully identified the relevant HTML elements. Questions reside within `div[role="listitem"]`, and their text is found in `div[data-testid="Question Text"]`. Answer options are within `div[data-testid="MCQ Answers"]`, with each option as a `section`. Correct answers are marked by an SVG icon, either `use xlink:href="#check"` or `AssemblyIcon`. Image URLs, when present, are in `div.Image-image`. I'm now manually extracting and formatting the data, aiming for integration with `data.ts`. I plan to handle up to 57 questions if context allows.


**Refining Question Parsing**

I'm now processing several new questions and their associated data. I'm focusing on accurately extracting the question text, answer options, and the correct answer. The category will be "Chapter 1" for each of them. I've encountered different variations in wording for the questions, as well as distinct layouts. Ensuring consistent and reliable data extraction is a priority.


**Examining Data Structure**

My focus is shifting to how to accurately map the HTML data into the `questions` array. I'm wrestling with question 6, and I see I need to handle image references, especially when the question itself doesn't contain an image but references the options.     id: "q-joint-43",
    questionText: "Explain why the shoulder joint is both more mobile and weaker than the hip joint.",
    answerText: "The shoulder joint has a shallow socket (the glenoid fossa) into which the head of the humerus fits, and the labrum only slightly deepens the socket. The head of the humerus is larger in relation to the socket. It is deepened by a number of ligaments, but because of its structure, tends to be weaker than the hip joint. It is highly movable and can rotate easily. The hip joint has a deeper socket (acetabulum) into which the head of the femur inserts and is also strengthened by ligaments. Because of the angle at which the femur fits into its socket, along with the depth of the socket, the hip is a stronger, less mobile joint, made to support the weight of the body.",

payload.text:
ضيف هي الاسئلة chapter 1 واي سؤال مكرار احذف وعدل كل الاسئلة سوي نفس نص امتحانات الجامعة
Terms in this set (140)
atom
unit of matter that makes up a chemical element, contains a nucleus (with protons and neutrons) and electrons in shells orbiting around the nucleus
molecule
a combination of two or more atoms that share electrons
compound
composed of 2 or more separate elements
cell
basic structural and functional unit of an element; the smallest structure capable of performing all activities vital to life
tissue
a group of similar cells and their intercellular substance joined together to perform a specific function
organ
a structure composed of two or more kinds of tissues with a specific function and usually a recognizable shape
organ system
an association of organs that have a common function
organism
a total living form; one individual
anatomy
the study of the structure of the body and the relationship of its parts to each other
physiology
science that deals with the functions of an organism or its parts
anatomical position
a position of the body universally used in anatomical descriptions in which the body is erect, the head is level, the eyes face forward, the upper limbs are at the sides, the palms face forward, and the feet are flat on the floor
superior
toward the head or upper part of a structure
inferior
away from the head or toward the lower part of the structure
anterior
nearer to or at the front of the body
posterior
nearer to or at the back of the body
medial
nearer to the midline
lateral
farther from the midline
proximal
nearer to the attachment of a limb to the trunk; nearer to the origination of a structure
distal
farther from the attachment of a limb to the trunk; father from the origination of a structure
supine
body lying face up
prone
body lying face down
ipsilateral
on the same side of the body as another structure
contralateral
on the opposite side of the body from another structure
superficial
toward or on the surface of the body
deep
away from the surface of the body
ventral
front or anterior on human; usually used for animals (relating to the underside (belly) of an animal)
dorsal
back or posterior on humans; usually used for animals (relating to the back of an animal)
sagittal
a plane that divides the body or organ into left and right portions. Can be midsagittal (equal divisions) or parasagittal (unequal divisions)
parasagittal
a vertical plane that does not pass through the midline and that divides the body or organs into UNEQUAL left and right portions
midsagittal
a vertical plane that passes through the midline of the body that divides the body or organs into EQUAL left and right sides. Also called a median plane
transverse
a plane that divides the body into superior and inferior portions. Also called a cross-sectional or horizontal plane
frontal/ coronal
a plane at a right angle to the midsagittal plane that divides the body or organs into anterior and posterior portions. Also called a coronal plane
homeostasis
the maintenance of relatively stable conditions in the body's internal environment produced by the interplay of all of the body's regulatory processes
feedback system
cycle of events in which the status of the body condition is monitored, evaluated, changed, remonitored, and reevaluated
stimulus
any stress that changes a controlled condition; any change in the internal or external environment that excites a sensory receptor, a neuron, or a muscle fiber
receptor
a specialized cell or a distal portion of a neuron that responds to a specific sensory modality, such as touch, pressure, cold, light, or sound, and converts it to an electrical signal (generator or receptor potential). A specific molecule or cluster of molecules that recognizes and binds a particular ligand.
control center
part of a feedback system that sets the range of values within which a controlled condition should be maintained, evaluates input from receptors, and generates output commands
negative feedback
a feedback system that reverses a change in a controlled condition
positive feedback
a feedback system that strengthens a change in one of the body's controlled conditions
pericardium
a loose-fitting membrane that encloses the heart, consisting of a superficial fibrous layer and a deep serous layer
peritoneum
the largest serous membrane of the body that lines the abominable cavity and covers the viscera within it
levels of structural organization of the human body
chemical level, cellular level, tissue level, organ level, system level, organismal level
list the organ systems
integumentary system, skeletal system, muscular system, nervous system, endocrine system, cardiovascular system, lymphatic and immune system, respiratory system, digestive system, urinary system, reproductive system
list the basic life processes
metabolism, responsiveness, movement, growth, differentiation, reproduction
catabolism
the breakdown of complex chemical substances into simpler components
anabolism
the building up of complex chemical substances from smaller, simpler components
metabolism
the sum of all chemical processes that occur in the body
intracellular fluid
the fluid within the cells
extracellular fluid
the fluid outside the body cells
interstitial fluid
the extracellular fluid that fills the narrow spaces between the cells of tissues
List the components of the integumentary system
skin, hair, fingernails, toenails, sweat glands, oil glands
list the functions of the integumentary system
protects body, helps regulate body temperature, eliminates some wastes, helps make vitamin D, detects sensations such as touch, pain, warmth, and cold, stores fat and provides insulation
list the components of the skeletal system
bones and joints, cartilages
list the functions of the skeletal system
supports and protects body, provides surface area for muscle attachments, aids body movements, houses cells that produce blood cells, stores minerals and lipids
list the components of the muscular system
skeletal muscle tissue
list the functions of the muscular system
participates in body movements, maintains posture, produces heat
list the components of the nervous system
brain, spinal cord, nerves, sense organs like eyes and ears
list the functions of the nervous system
generates action potentials (nerve impulses) to regulate body activity, detects changes in body's internal and external environments, interprets changes and responds by causing muscular contractions or glandular secretions
list the components of the endocrine system
hormone producing glands (pineal gland, hypothalamus, pituitary gland, thymus, thyroid gland, parathyroid gland, adrenal gland, pancreas, ovaries, and testes), hormone producing cells in several other organs
list the functions of the endocrine system
regulates body activities by releasing hormones
list the components of the cardiovascular system
blood, heart, blood vessels
list the functions of the cardiovascular system
heart pumps blood through blood vessels; blood carries oxygen and nutrients to cells and carbon dioxide and wastes away from cells and helps regulate acid-base balance, temperature, and water content of body fluids; blood components help defend against disease and repair damaged blood vessels
list the components of the lymphatic and immune system
lymphatic fluid and vessels, spleen, thymus, lymph nodes, tonsils, cells that carry out immune responses (B cells, T cells, etc.)
list the functions of the lymphatic and immune system
returns proteins and fluid to blood; carries lipids from gastrointestinal tract to blood; contains sites of maturation and proliferation of B cells and T cells that protect against disease-causing microbes
list the components of the respiratory system
lungs, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchial tubes
list the functions of the respiratory system
transfers oxygen from inhaled air to blood and carbon dioxide from blood to exhaled air; helps regulate acid-base balance of body fluids; air flowing out of lungs through vocal cords produces sounds
list the components of the digestive system
organs of the gastrointestinal tract and accessory organs of digestion (mouth, pharynx, esophagus, stomach, small and large intestines, anus, salivary glands, liver, gallbladder, pancreas
list the functions of the digestive system
achieves physical and chemical breakdown of food, absorbs nutrients, eliminates solid wastes
list the components of the urinary system
kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder, urethra
list the functions of the urinary system
produces, stores, and eliminates urine; eliminates wastes and regulates volume and chemical composition of blood; helps maintain the acid-base balance of body fluids; maintains body's mineral balance; helps regulate production of red blood cells
list the components of the reproductive system
gonads (testes in males, ovaries in females). For women: uterine tubes, uterus, vagina, mammary glands. For men: epididymis, ductus (or vas) deferens, seminal vesicles, prostate, penis
list the body cavities
cranial cavity, vertebral cavity, thoracic cavity (split into pleural cavities, pericardial cavity, and mediastinum), abdominopelvic cavity (split into abdominal cavity and pelvic cavity)
Atoms essential for maintaining life
carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, phosphorus, calcium, sulfur
cellular level
molecules combine to form cells
types of tissue in the human body
epithelial tissue, connective tissue, muscular tissue, nervous tissue
organs in the cranial cavity
brain
organs in the vertebral canal
spinal cord and beginnings of spinal nerves
organs in the pleural cavity
a lung in each
organs in the thoracic cavity
lungs, heart, thymus, esophagus, trachea, several large blood vessels, bronchi
list the organs in the pericardial cavity
heart
list the organs in the mediastinum
heart, thymus, esophagus, trachea, several large blood vessels
list the organs in the abdominopelvic cavity
stomach, spleen, liver, gallbladder, small intestine, large intestine, urinary bladder, internal organs of reproduction
list the organs in the abdominal cavity
stomach, spleen, liver, gallbladder, small intestine, and most of the large intestine
list the organs in the pelvic cavity
urinary bladder, portions of the large intestine, and internal organs of reproduction
diaphragm
dome-shaped muscle that separates the thoracic cavity from the abdominopelvic cavity
visceral layer of the serous membrane
the layer that covers the organs
parietal layer of the serous membrane
the layer that clings to the wall of the cavities
name of the serous membrane around the lungs
pleura
name of the serous membrane around the heart
pericardium
name of the serous membrane in the abdominal cavity
peritoneum
viscera
organs inside the thoracic and abdominopelvic cavities
cephalic
head
cranial
skull
facial
face
frontal
forehead
temporal
temple
orbital
eye
ocular
eye
otic
ear
buccal

    answerText: "The shoulder joint has a shallow socket (the glenoid fossa) into which the head of the humerus fits, and the labrum only slightly deepens the socket. It is highly movable and can rotate easily. The hip joint has a deeper socket (acetabulum) into which the head of the femur inserts and is also strengthened by ligaments. **Reviewing Question Structures** I'm currently focused on the `data.ts` file, specifically the question structures within it. My primary aim is to develop the question options. I've noted the existing `options` arrays in many questions, but I'm now looking at the new additions to see how they utilize these arrays, ensuring consistency and exploring alternative approaches. **Analyzing Option Generation** I've examined the `Quiz.tsx` component and the `generateQuiz` function. It handles missing or insufficient `options` arrays by generating them. The logic shuffles existing options, ensuring the correct answer's inclusion, or if there's no pre-defined array, creates new options from the question pool. I'm now determining how best to streamline this within the main question structures. **Optimizing Option Creation** I'm now examining how the quiz options are being created and randomized. The `generateQuiz` function seems to use existing arrays if they are provided. However, I've noted a need for optimization in how it handles cases where these arrays are either partially defined or missing altogether. I'm focusing on ensuring robust randomization and preventing duplicate or overly similar options to create a more engaging experience. I'm aiming to balance programmatic generation with control provided through the data. **Addressing Option Presentation** I'm now grappling with the `options` array, especially for "list" and "describe" questions, which have empty arrays. The existing auto-generation creates long, hard-to-read options from other question's answers. I need to rethink how to handle these questions to provide a cleaner, more focused user experience. My current focus is to balance automatic generation with a more refined presentation of answers to these types of queries. **Considering UI Enhancements** I'm now back in `Quiz.tsx`, scrutinizing the button presentation. The current display using the `button` element looks pretty good with the icon and text arrangement. However, I'm considering if there are ways to enhance the visual presentation, perhaps with different styling or animation, to improve user engagement. I'm also thinking about incorporating accessibility features, making the options easier to navigate for all users. **Streamlining Distractor Generation** I'm now back in `Quiz.tsx`, reworking the `generateQuiz` function. My recent focus is the generation of question distractors. I'm prioritizing the quality and relevance of the options, especially for the "list" and "describe" questions with empty arrays in `data.ts`, like those in the "Joints" and "Muscular System" sections. I'm now working to filter generated answers by length for visual consistency, and also exploring category-based selection to enhance the relevance of distractors. I also started implementing keyboard shortcuts (1, 2, 3, 4) to navigate the options, aiming to make it easier to navigate. **Refining Distractor Relevance** I've returned to the `generateQuiz` function, specifically addressing the generation of distractor options. I've pinpointed an area for refinement: the current logic pulls distractors from the entire dataset, potentially leading to irrelevant options. I'm now implementing category-based filtering to ensure distractors are thematically aligned with the question's content, improving quiz difficulty and relevance. **Integrating Audio & Cards** I'm now revising the `Quiz.tsx` file, integrating a speaker icon for text-to-speech accessibility alongside each option. This will be available during practice and explanation modes. Simultaneously, I'm refactoring the option presentation to resemble card-like elements, with hover effects and improved typography, enhancing visual distinction and user engagement. **Developing Answer Presentation** I'm now implementing the sequential slide-in animation for quiz options, with an added numbering system (A, B, C, D) for clarity and to improve the overall flow. I have also added category-based filtering of distractors. This filters based on the `sameCategory` type filter. تم تحديث خيارات الأسئلة في نظام الاختبار لتصبح أكثر ذكاءً وجاذبية، مع مراعاة طلباتك بخصوص الصوت.

**أبرز التغييرات:**

1.  **خيارات ذكية (Smart Distractors):**
    *   تم تعديل المنطق البرمجي (Algorithm) لاختيار الإجابات الخاطئة من *نفس فئة السؤال* أولاً (مثلاً: سؤال عن العضلات سيظهر خيارات عضلات أخرى)، مما يجعل الاختبار أكثر صعوبة وفائدة بدلاً من ظهور خيارات عشوائية غير منطقية.

2.  **تحسين واجهة الخيارات (UI Design):**
    *   إضافة ترقيم حرفي **(A, B, C, D)** لكل خيار لسهولة التمييز.
    *   تصميم جديد للأزرار يشبه البطاقات، يتفاعل عند التحديد بشكل أوضح.

3.  **دعم الصوت في الخيارات (Practice Mode):**
    *   في وضع "التدريب" (Practice)، سيظهر زر مكبر صوت صغير بجانب كل خيار عند تمرير الماوس عليه، لقراءة الخيار بشكل منفرد (للمساعدة في النطق).
    *   تم التأكد من أن هذا الزر **لا يظهر في وضع الامتحان (Exam Mode)** التزاماً بطلبك السابق.

4.  **اختصارات لوحة المفاتيح:**
    *   يمكنك الآن اختيار الإجابة باستخدام أرقام لوحة المفاتيح (1, 2, 3, 4) لسرعة الحل.

    questionText: "Explain why the shoulder joint is both more mobile and weaker than the hip joint.",
    answerText: "The shoulder joint has a shallow socket (the glenoid fossa) into which the head of the humerus fits, and the labrum only slightly deepens the socket. The head of the humerus is larger in relation to the socket. It is deepened by a number of ligaments, but because of its structure, tends to be weaker than the hip joint. It is highly movable and can rotate easily. The hip joint has a deeper socket (acetabulum) into which the head of the femur inserts and is also strengthened by ligaments. Because of the angle at which the femur fits into its socket, along with the depth of the socket, the hip is a stronger, less mobile joint, made to support the weight of the body.",

payload.text: طورة النظام الصوتي سوي احسن واضح

**Revising the Approach**

I've been analyzing the user request and breaking down the implications.  The core goal is clearly audio enhancement, and the user's feedback, though concise, points toward a need for improved clarity and overall quality. I'm now exploring specific areas within the audio system to target based on these initial insights, and considering the best methods to achieve this.


**Refining the Audio Solution**

I'm now zeroing in on the audio enhancement strategy. It's clear that the built-in browser voices aren't cutting it, and I need a better solution for clearer pronunciation, especially for medical terms. I'm exploring methods to smooth the transitions when speech is interrupted due to state changes. My research has led me to consider incorporating an offline TTS solution, provided it does not need a backend API.


**Prioritizing Speech Synthesis**

I'm now prioritizing the existing `speechSynthesis` API for instant feedback. API keys and asynchronous fetching felt too clunky for a fluid user experience. My focus is now on refining this existing approach for a smoother, higher-quality audio output. This involves exploring better handling of interruptions and optimizing pronunciation. I'm also considering slowing down the rate of speech for complex terms to improve comprehension.


**Implementing Key Adjustments**

I'm currently working to integrate several key adjustments to enhance the audio quality. First, I'm setting a fixed 'en-US' language and slightly reducing the speech rate for improved clarity. I'm also planning to refactor the speech logic into a custom `useSpeech` hook for better code organization. While perfect pronunciation of complex medical terms is a challenge without SSML, I'm trying to improve the text itself (pauses) to help. Visual feedback like pulsing buttons will also be added.


**Exploring Offline Alternatives**

I'm now revisiting the architectural decisions around audio generation. The potential benefits of using the Gemini API are significant, primarily for audio quality. However, the associated latency could severely impact the user experience, particularly within the rapid-fire quiz format. Currently, I'm focusing on enhancing the existing `window.speechSynthesis` functionality. The goal is to provide a "Pro" version of the browser-based TTS by centralizing speech logic into a custom hook. I'm exploring ways to select the most optimal voices and pre-process challenging medical terms for improved pronunciation. UI feedback, like visual equalizers, will also be incorporated.


**Consolidating the Implementation**

I'm now actively focusing on integrating the `useSpeech` hook across the key components (`Quiz.tsx`, `Flashcards.tsx`, and `QuestionList.tsx`). I'm also adding a speaking indicator for visual feedback. Further, I'm adjusting the speech rate to 0.9 for improved clarity, and prioritizing voices in the following order: Google US English, Microsoft Zira, and finally Samantha. I have a clear plan for the hook's structure.  This involves voice loading logic, and speak/cancel functions.


**Refactoring for Clarity**

I'm now fully immersed in the core implementation, streamlining the audio logic with the `useSpeech` hook. This involved incorporating the hook into key components like `Quiz.tsx`. The significant shift is toward a specific voice selection strategy, prioritizing Google's US English or Microsoft's Zira to avoid robotic default voices. I've also reduced the speech rate slightly to 0.9 for better comprehension of complex terms.


**Implementing Further Refinements**

I've just added the `useSpeech` hook to `Flashcards.tsx` and `QuestionList.tsx`, completing the integration across core components. I've also added visual feedback – a speaking indicator. Further, I've now tweaked the voice selection logic, making it prioritize "Google US English", followed by "Microsoft Zira" and "Samantha," defaulting to any US English voice. I've slightly reduced the speech rate to 0.9 for improved clarity. Finally, I'm working on a "Waveform" icon to show visual audio playing feedback.


**Visualizing Audio Feedback**

I'm now focusing on integrating visual feedback for audio. I've decided to use a `Volume2` icon with an animation class, but I'll use a full `AudioWaveform` if available.  I'm also ensuring the correct structure in the key files. I'm taking care to retain the "Smart Distractors" logic from the previous update in the `Quiz.tsx` file while merging in the new audio hook. I'll need to check for `window` availability for `speechSynthesis` and handle the `onvoiceschanged` event correctly. Finally, I'll add a visual "reading" state for when the text is actively spoken.


**Consolidating Audio Management**

I'm now fully replacing internal audio state across components with the `useSpeech` hook for cleaner code.  `Flashcards.tsx` and `QuestionList.tsx` are refactored. I'm focusing on the integration of the hook. I'll maintain component logic, so the audio hook won't be in `types.ts`. I'm using component-specific typing or defining in hook to minimize file changes.

لقد قمت بتطوير النظام الصوتي بالكامل. بدلاً من الاعتماد على الأكواد المتناثرة في كل صفحة، قمت بإنشاء "محرك صوتي" مركزي (Custom Hook) يسمى `useSpeech`.

**أبرز التحسينات في النظام الصوتي الجديد:**

1.  **نطق أوضح (Enhanced Clarity):** تم ضبط سرعة الكلام (Rate) تلقائياً لتكون أبطأ قليلاً (0.9x) مما يجعل المصطلحات الطبية أكثر وضوحاً وسهولة في الفهم.
2.  **اختيار ذكي للصوت (Smart Voice Selection):** النظام الآن يبحث تلقائياً عن أفضل الأصوات البشرية المتاحة في المتصفح (مثل "Google US English" أو "Microsoft Zira" أو "Samantha") بدلاً من الصوت الروبوتي الافتراضي.
3.  **إدارة التداخل (Conflict Management):** يمنع النظام تداخل الأصوات. إذا ضغطت على زر تشغيل جديد، يتوقف الصوت السابق فوراً ويبدأ الجديد.
4.  **تأثيرات بصرية (Visual Feedback):** الأزرار الآن تتفاعل بشكل "نابض" (Pulse Animation) أثناء القراءة لتعرف بالضبط ما يتم قراءته.

payload.text:
Fix the following errors:

* `Speech synthesis error
[object Object]`

**Analyzing Speech Error**

I'm currently investigating a "Speech synthesis error [object Object]" reported in the console. It seems this error typically arises when `speechSynthesis.speak()` is executed with issues, or when the `utterance.onerror` event is triggered. I'm focusing on these two possibilities to pinpoint the root cause.


**Debugging Error Handling**

I've examined the `hooks/useSpeech.ts` file and found the error handling uses `onerror` for speech synthesis failures. Logging the `SpeechSynthesisErrorEvent` directly produces the generic `[object Object]` output, so I'm digging into specific error scenarios. My working hypothesis involves common issues like interrupted speech from `speechSynthesis.cancel()`, and considering auto-play policy constraints that might arise unexpectedly, and remote voice network related errors.


**Pinpointing Root Causes**

I'm now investigating potential causes for the speech synthesis errors, focusing on scenarios where interruptions or cancellations are involved. The initial hypothesis points to the `cancel()` function causing `onerror` triggers due to immediate speech termination, but I'm looking into edge cases like very long texts or network instabilities, especially since `cancel()` stops *all* speech.


**Refining Error Logging**

I'm refining my error handling strategy to exclude benign interruptions from the error logs.  Specifically, I'm examining the `onerror` event within `hooks/useSpeech.ts` and filtering out 'interrupted' or 'canceled' errors to prevent unnecessary logging. If the `SpeechSynthesisErrorEvent` is 'interrupted' or 'canceled', the speech state variables are reset and we proceed without logging. I'm exploring all ways the user might encounter an error to ensure that any issue is handled appropriately, particularly edge cases not yet considered.


**Optimizing Speech Queueing**

The 'interrupted' and 'canceled' errors stem directly from the `cancel()` function, as expected, confirming the initial hypothesis. My current focus is refining the `onerror` handler in `useSpeech.ts` to exclude these benign errors. Additionally, I'm now exploring the implications of removing the immediate `cancel()` call within the `speak` function to leverage the inherent queueing capabilities of `speechSynthesis`, potentially improving reliability, since the old code will cancel all speech. This will require further testing.


**Refining Error Filtering**

I'm now revising my error handling to address the logging of anticipated interruptions. The core modification centers on filtering within the `onerror` event handler of `hooks/useSpeech.ts`. Specifically, I'm checking the `event.error` property of the `SpeechSynthesisErrorEvent`, excluding logging if it's 'interrupted' or 'canceled'. This prevents the expected behavior, which occurs on speech interruption, from generating spurious console output. I'm also addressing a potential race condition and ensuring that the correct error event is always processed, especially after using `cancel()`.


**Examining State Synchronization**

I'm now investigating potential conflicts between the `cancel()` function and the `onstart` event within the `speak` function. Specifically, I'm concerned that synchronous state updates from `cancel()` might interfere with the subsequent `onstart` event handler, leading to flickering in the `isSpeaking` state. The immediate `cancel()` call within `speak` sets `isSpeaking` to false, which might be overwritten by the later `onstart` event setting it to true. Further analysis is needed to confirm the exact sequence of events and ensure smooth state transitions.


**Analyzing Error Sequencing**

I'm now zeroing in on a critical bug related to speech state transitions and error handling. I've realized that if a previous utterance's `onerror` triggers *after* a new `onstart` event, it inadvertently sets the `isSpeaking` state to false, prematurely ending the new speech. To fix this, I'm integrating the `activeUtteranceId` ref into the `onerror` handler, ensuring that only errors associated with the *currently* active utterance are processed. The `cancel()` function resetting `activeUtteranceId` also must be carefully handled for this to work.


**Finalizing State Management**

I'm now revising my approach to state management and error handling within `useSpeech.ts` to fully address the race condition. I realize that relying on `activeUtteranceId` alone isn't sufficient, as an old utterance's `onerror` can still interfere. The solution involves assigning a unique internal ID to each utterance and verifying it against the current active ID within the event handlers. This ensures that only events related to the currently active utterance affect the component's state, preventing the issues I've observed related to out-of-order events. The updated code will guarantee that state changes align precisely with the intended speech output.


**Enhancing State Integrity**

I'm now implementing the final refinement to address the race condition, ensuring that state transitions are robust and consistent, regardless of event order. The core of my solution is in `useSpeech.ts` with a focus on uniquely identifying each utterance and associating it with events. My approach involves verifying that an event corresponds to the currently active utterance using `activeUtteranceId.current === id` before it modifies the component's state. I'm carefully managing utterance IDs within `speak`, `onstart`, `onend` and `onerror` to guarantee that all event handlers correctly interact. This will effectively eliminate all observed state inconsistencies.


**Finalizing Event Handling**

I'm now implementing the final refinement to address the race condition, ensuring that state transitions are robust and consistent, regardless of event order. The core of my solution is in `useSpeech.ts` with a focus on uniquely identifying each utterance and associating it with events. My approach involves verifying that an event corresponds to the currently active utterance using `activeUtteranceId.current === id` before it modifies the component's state. I'm carefully managing utterance IDs within `speak`, `onstart`, `onend` and `onerror` to guarantee that all event handlers correctly interact. This will effectively eliminate all observed state inconsistencies.


**Addressing Race Conditions**

I've been focused on preventing state inconsistencies caused by the interaction of speech cancellation and error events. The primary challenge stems from ensuring that the `onerror` event for a cancelled utterance doesn't inadvertently affect the state of the currently active speech. I've now resolved this issue by verifying the event's associated utterance ID within all handlers. This ensures that only events related to the currently active utterance affect the component's state, preventing the issues I've observed. All events correctly interact, including speech start, end, and errors.


**Verifying Event Association**

I'm now putting the finishing touches on the `useSpeech.ts` file, ensuring state transitions are robust. The key is in verifying the associated utterance ID within the `onerror` event handler. The cleanup in `useEffect` is correct. I'm leveraging the unique `activeUtteranceId` and checking that an event relates to the *current* utterance before changing any state. This will prevent race conditions between old and new events.


**Improving Voice Selection**

I'm now refining the voice selection logic. I'm prioritizing US English voices, favoring specific named voices and local/offline options for enhanced clarity and speed. If those aren't available, I'll fall back to any US English voice. The cleanup `cancel()` call also avoids errors on unmount.


**Prioritizing Voice Options**

I'm now making final adjustments to the voice selection mechanism. It prioritizes US English voices, favoring specific ones and local or offline options to improve clarity and reduce latency. Failing that, any available US English voice will be used. The cleanup `cancel()` call also avoids errors on unmount.


**Refining Speech Synthesis**

I'm now revising my approach to text processing for improved pronunciation. I've added subtle pauses after punctuation marks, enhancing the flow of speech. I've also implemented voice selection logic, prioritizing US English voices and local/offline options for optimal quality and speed. I'm ensuring that speech events only impact current utterances and that the component's state remains consistent.


**Enhancing Speech Processing**

I'm now revising my approach to text processing for improved pronunciation, enhancing the flow of speech, and ensuring that any potential errors are handled correctly, and the states are consistent, which is paramount. I'm prioritizing the `onerror` event within the `useEffect` and confirming it is for the current utterance.

To fix the speech synthesis error and improve robustness, I will update the `useSpeech` hook to handle interruption errors gracefully (preventing them from logging to the console) and improve state management to avoid race conditions when switching between audio tracks quickly.

payload.text: طورة النظام الصوتي خلي يكون افضل

**Prioritizing Audio Enhancement**

I've been focusing on enhancing the application's audio capabilities. The current `useSpeech` hook is already quite robust, with intelligent voice selection logic. It prioritizes high-quality voices like "Google US English" and "Microsoft Zira" and supports rate adjustments for speech output, now I want to further enhance this functionality.


**Refining Voice Control**

I'm presently zeroing in on making the voice control even better. We're thinking the voice selection could be smarter, maybe allowing user selection as a fallback. The rate, currently at 0.9, might benefit from being configurable for clarity. Text pre-processing could use some polish too.


**Enhancing Text Handling**

Right now, I'm focusing on text preprocessing to improve clarity. Sentence breaking and pauses are key.  I'm adding pauses after periods and commas for natural phrasing. To make it reliable, I'll split long texts into chunks for more consistent speech output in Chrome, and check for `speechSynthesis` availability.


**Updating Text Processing**

I'm currently updating the `useSpeech.ts` functionality. The `speak` function now cancels current speech, preprocesses text with replacements, splits it into chunks based on punctuation, and queues these chunks to manage speech state. My goal is to maintain a preferred voice selection and refine error handling to keep user interface coherent.


**Revising Chunking Logic**

The chunking will now be simplified to split by sentences and enqueue them in `useSpeech`. I've decided against a full queue due to complexity. Focus remains on native `speechSynthesis` queue with cleaner text and optimal voice settings. I'm prioritizing "Clear" with slower rates and explicit pauses, and addressing robustness by sentence chunking. This should address the user's request.


**Refining Queue Management**

I'm tackling the 15-second timeout bug by revising the `useSpeech.ts` logic. The `speak` function now sanitizes text, splits it into sentences, and creates utterances for each, relying on the native queue. I'm focusing on accurately tracking the entire sequence's completion with `isSpeaking` using utterance counting, as the initial approach was problematic. The plan now includes an `utteranceCount` ref and active utterance ID tracking for robust state management.


**Optimizing Speech Sequencing**

I'm currently revising the `speak` function to address issues with the native queue. I'll split the text into sentences, create an utterance for each, and configure the voice and rate. To manage state, the `onstart` event will trigger `isSpeaking(true)` only for the first utterance, and the `onend` event will signal completion (i.e., set `isSpeaking(false)`) on the final utterance. This approach relies on tracking the "active" utterance ID. Additionally, canceling will clear the entire queue, as expected. I've considered that the queue is global to the browser, and the native API manages the play order by default, and this simplifies the `useSpeech.ts` implementation.


**Streamlining Speech Logic**

Currently, I'm integrating the `splitText` function into `useSpeech.ts` to divide text into manageable sentences using the provided regular expression. Additionally, I'm reviewing the voice selection strategy, considering `en-GB` as a fallback preference. Finally, I'm maintaining UI responsiveness by ensuring speech feedback continues to be prioritized.


**Updating Voice Control Further**

I'm now revising the `useSpeech.ts` hook. I'm focusing on cleaning up the implementation. I've decided not to change `Quiz.tsx`, and I've set the voice rate to 0.85 for clarity. I'm also addressing the `cancel` function to ensure it properly resets the speech state. I'll maintain the `onvoiceschanged` logic to handle voice loading. Finally, I've confirmed existing imports like `useSpeech` in `Quiz`, `Flashcards`, and `QuestionList` remain intact.


**Finalizing Voice Preferences**

Right now, I'm focusing on the voice selection within the `useSpeech.ts` hook. I've expanded the preference list for clarity, and it now prioritizes voices like "Google US English", "Samantha", and "Microsoft Zira Desktop." The goal is to provide a sensible fallback if the most preferred voices are unavailable.


**Enhancing Cancel Functionality**

I'm now revising the `cancel` function to comprehensively halt speech. I'll include additional checks to ensure proper state resets, setting `isSpeaking` to false and clearing the `speakingId`, `activeType`, and `activeUtteranceId`. This meticulous approach ensures that voice commands respond as expected, providing a smooth user experience.


**Adjusting Utterance Management**

Currently, I'm refactoring the utterance management within `useSpeech.ts` to improve performance and responsiveness. I'm focusing on refining the event handling within the `speak` function. I'm replacing the `onend` and `onerror` event handling with more robust checking to prevent interruptions and ensure a seamless speech output experience.


**Finalizing Error Handling**

I'm now integrating error handling for speech synthesis, specifically within the `onerror` event handler.  I've added robust checks to ensure the visual state aligns with speech events.  My goal is to prevent any lingering visual effects or inconsistent feedback to the user, particularly during errors.


**Integrating Robust Preprocessing**

I'm now integrating robust text preprocessing, especially for anatomical terms. I'm focusing on handling symbols like `/`, `&` and breaking text into sentences for reliable Chrome performance and natural pauses. Error handling has been updated to suppress console noise caused by `cancel()` events. I'm updating `useSpeech.ts` now.


**Finalizing Voice Prioritization**

I'm now implementing the voice prioritization logic within `useSpeech.ts`. I'm focusing on creating an accurate list of preferred voices, including "Google US English," "Samantha," and "Microsoft Zira Desktop." This will ensure a high-quality speech output with minimal latency and correct pronunciation, and will work as a fallback.


**Prioritizing Sentence Segmentation**

I'm now focusing on further simplifying text preparation for speech. I've integrated sentence segmentation using a regular expression within the `speak` function. I'm also ensuring the `cancel` function halts the speech output, with consistent state reset after any cancellation. The goal is to provide reliable, clear, and well-paced speech.



**Streamlining Text to Speech**

I'm now implementing the `speak` function within `useSpeech.ts`. It'll process the text, replacing symbols and ensuring punctuation pauses, splitting it into manageable sentences. The initial approach using `speechSynthesis` has proven more reliable than the queued approach. I'm focusing on improved sentence segmentation.


**Enhancing Utterance Management**

Currently, I'm adjusting the utterance event listeners for the `speak` function. I'm streamlining the `onend` callback to ensure it correctly manages the speech state and resets `isSpeaking` only when the active utterance finishes. The `onerror` handler will handle any errors during speech synthesis and reset the state. This should provide a more robust and predictable speech output.


**Finalizing Error Robustness**

I'm now implementing the `onerror` event listener within the `speak` function of `useSpeech.ts`.  It'll handle various error scenarios, providing a detailed console log and ensuring `isSpeaking` is set to `false`, effectively resetting the speech state. I'll maintain careful checking to prevent incorrect visual feedback.

تم تحديث النظام الصوتي (`Speech Engine`) ليصبح أكثر احترافية ووضوحاً، مع إصلاح مشكلة ظهور الأخطاء (`Speech synthesis error`) في وحدة التحكم (Console).

**أبرز التحسينات التي تمت:**

1.  **نطق ذكي للرموز (Smart Preprocessing):**
    *   يتم الآن تحويل الرموز مثل `/` إلى "or"، و `&` إلى "and"، و `+` إلى "plus"، والنطاقات الرقمية `10-20` إلى "10 to 20". هذا يجعل القراءة طبيعية ومفهومة جداً للمصطلحات الطبية.
2.  **تقسيم النص (Chunking):**
    *   تم تقسيم النصوص الطويلة إلى جمل منفصلة. هذا يحل مشكلة توقف الصوت فجأة في متصفح Google Chrome عند قراءة نصوص طويلة، ويضيف وقفات طبيعية للتنفس بين الجمل.
3.  **إصلاح الأخطاء (Error Handling):**
    *   تم منع ظهور خطأ `[object Object]` عند مقاطعة القراءة (عند الضغط على زر "Stop" أو تشغيل صوت آخر)، حيث يتعامل النظام الآن مع المقاطعة كحدث طبيعي وليس كخطأ.
4.  **سرعة ووضوح:**
    *   تم ضبط السرعة على `0.9` لضمان نطق المصطلحات التشريحية بوضوح تام دون أن تكون بطيئة جداً.

النظام الآن يعمل بسلاسة وموثوقية عالية.

entries.description: Enhanced speech engine with sentence chunking for reliability, text preprocessing for better pronunciation of symbols, and improved error handling to suppress interruption logs.

payload.text: قصدي الصوة

**Interpreting User Intent**

I'm now zeroing in on the user's focus on the voice quality itself, or at least they are making it clear that the audio aspect is the key thing. The phrasing "قصدي الصوة" has shifted the emphasis.


**Refining Voice Selection**

I've been thinking about the voice selection strategy, and it's clear I need to enhance it. My current approach looks for specific voice names, but I can improve by searching for keywords like "Natural" or "Premium" in the voice name. I should also dynamically adjust rate and pitch based on the selected voice type. I'll make sure it handles cases where the ideal voice isn't available with graceful fallback. And making sure to force English.


**Implementing Voice Priorities**

I'm now prioritizing voices based on their perceived quality. I'm focusing on "Natural," "Premium," or "Enhanced" voices and specific defaults like "Google US English," "Samantha," and "Microsoft Zira/David." I'm also adjusting the speech rate depending on the voice's nature: natural voices get a standard rate, while standard ones get a slightly slower rate for clarity. I'm also ensuring the language is English, and specifically, that I'm checking for en-US or en-GB, in that order, to set up the best voice.


**Optimizing Voice Delivery**

I'm currently focused on the voice delivery process. I've now added more voices to my prioritized list. The aim is to get a natural-sounding voice by default. I'm prioritizing voices with "Natural" or "Premium" in their names. I am also adjusting the speech rate, so that the natural ones speak at a slightly faster pace for a smoother delivery. I'm focusing on "Google US English," "Samantha," and other US English options, and I will be sure to also handle a fallback.


**Enhancing Voice Quality**

I'm now thinking about how to refine the voice selection even more. My main focus is to prioritize natural and clear voices, specifically the "Natural" voices often available on Edge/Azure platforms. If those aren't available, I'll fall back to "Google US English" or "Samantha." I've also decided to prioritize US English over UK English to maintain consistency in pronunciations, but I will consider clear UK voices if needed. I'm tweaking the pitch slightly too, aiming for a more engaging sound, but keeping it safe. And to make sure I don't get the same text read with a different voice later during the session, I'll ensure I handle `onvoiceschanged` properly.


**Adjusting Voice Chunking**

I'm now considering the voice chunking strategy and whether it disrupts the natural flow of voices, particularly the ones that handle punctuation well.  I will probably keep the chunking but relax the regex or just keep it as is, because Chrome often times out on long texts, regardless of voice quality. I'll focus on the absolute best voice available, by ranking voices based on "Natural", Google US English, Samantha, and Microsoft Zira, in that order, and handling any local en-US services as a last resort. Let's start coding the XML!

فهمت عليك، بدك **خامة الصوت** نفسها تكون طبيعية أكثر وأقرب للصوت البشري (Less Robotic).

المشكلة أن المتصفحات افتراضياً تختار أصواتاً عشوائية أو قديمة. قمت بتحديث الكود للبحث بذكاء عن الأصوات المصنفة كـ **"Natural"** أو **"Premium"** أو **"Enhanced"** المتوفرة في أنظمة التشغيل الحديثة (مثل Edge Browser, macOS, Android).

كما قمت بضبط السرعة بناءً على نوع الصوت: إذا وجدنا صوتاً طبيعياً، نتركه بسرعته العادية ليكون سلساً، وإذا كان صوتاً عادياً، نبطئه قليلاً ليكون أوضح.

إليك التحديث:

payload.text: الصوت سوي اوضح

**Enhancing Clarity in Synthesis**

I've been working on refining the speech synthesis, prioritizing voice selection for "Natural," "Premium," and "Enhanced" options. Long texts are now chunked for better stability, and text cleaning is also in place.


**Improving Speech Synthesis Clarity**

I'm now focusing on further enhancing clarity, especially for medical terms. I'm exploring rate adjustments, considering slightly slower speeds for all voices, even natural ones, to clarify anatomy vocabulary. I'm also adding more preprocessing steps, focusing on replacing common anatomical abbreviations like "fig." and improving punctuation handling for better pauses. The existing voice selection is good, but I'll make sure it strictly selects "en-US" voices when possible. I'm also trying to incorporate more explicit pauses.


**Optimizing Audio Output**

I'm now prioritizing clarity for all voices, as requested. I've already implemented "Natural" voice selection and chunking, but I'm further refining the output. I'm focusing on text preprocessing and explicit pauses to ensure crisp sound. I'm carefully reviewing the code to improve voice clarity, especially for those who need it.


**Adjusting Speech Delivery**

I'm now prioritizing clarity for all voices, as requested. I've adjusted the rate; high-quality voices are at 0.9, and standard voices at 0.85, to improve pronunciation, especially for medical terms.  I'm also spacing punctuation to ensure pauses and enunciation. I'm focusing on ensuring enunciation is improved across the output.


**Prioritizing Speech Precision**

I'm now focusing on slowing down the speech further to improve clarity. I'm ensuring all voices are set to 'en-US' to avoid accent issues.  I've added spaces around parentheses and I'm further refining punctuation handling, which will involve ensuring proper pauses.  The rate is now strictly controlled: 0.8 for high-quality voices and 0.75 for standard ones.  I am trying to improve the processing.


**Refining Audio Processing**

I'm now prioritizing clarity for all voices, adjusting for rate and punctuation to ensure proper pauses. I'm focusing on text preprocessing, specifically replacing special characters and applying smart rate logic. For standard voices that may be selected as a fallback, I've lowered the rate.  I've refined the `useSpeech` hook to ensure the best possible audio output, especially for complex scientific terminology.


**Refining Voice Prioritization**

I've been working on refining the voice loading logic within the `useSpeech` hook. I will prioritize Google US English, but now prefer Edge over generic "Natural". I'll consider these options while avoiding repeating the same voice. I need to make sure the audio is the best.


**Improving Voice Clarity Further**

I'm now prioritizing clarity for all voices, as requested. I've adjusted the rate; high-quality voices are at 0.9, and standard voices at 0.85, to improve pronunciation, especially for medical terms.  I'm focusing on ensuring enunciation is improved across the output, so I'll be updating `useSpeech.ts` soon. The rate control should provide the clarity that is needed.

لجعل الصوت أكثر وضوحاً ودقة في نطق المصطلحات الطبية، قمت بإجراء التعديلات التالية:

1.  **تقليل السرعة (Slower Rate):** تم تخفيض سرعة القراءة قليلاً (`0.9` للأصوات الطبيعية و `0.85` للأصوات العادية). هذا يمنح المستمع وقتاً كافياً لاستيعاب المصطلحات المعقدة دون أن يشعر بالملل.
2.  **تحسين الوقفات (Better Pausing):** يتم الآن استبدال الأقواس `()` بفواصل خفيفة، مما يجعل القارئ يتوقف قليلاً عند قراءة المعلومات الفرعية بدلاً من دمجها مع النص الأساسي.
3.  **مطابقة اللغة (Language Matching):** ضمان تطابق لغة النطق مع لغة الصوت المختار (en-US) لتجنب أي لكنات غريبة قد تظهر إذا اختار المتصفح صوتاً افتراضياً مختلفاً.

هذه التعديلات ستجعل الصوت "أوضح" ومفهوماً أكثر.

payload.text:
ضيف هي الاسئلة chapter 3
Terms in this set (91)
What is the smallest living unit of the body?
Cell
What are the three fundamentals of Cell Theory?
1) Cells are the building blocks of all plants and animals
2) All new cells come from division of preexisting cells
3 Cells are the smallest living units that perform all vital functions
All cells are descendants from what?
A single cell: The fertilized ovum
The process of gradual specialization of cells is known as what?
Cellular differentiation
What are the four primary types of tissue?
Epithelial, Connective, Muscle, Nervous
Cells are surrounded by a watery medium known as what?
Extracellular fluid aka interstitial fluid
The fluid inside of a cell is known as what?
Intracellular fluid aka cytosol
What structure separates the intracellular fluid and the extracellular fluid?
Plasma membrane
The intracellular structures within the sell with specific functions are known as what?
Organelles ("organs of the cells")
The cytosol + organelles is known as what?
Cytoplasm
Which organelle functions to break down organic compounds and neutralize compounds?
Peroxisome
Which organelle functions to break down organic compounds and damaged organelles or pathogens?
Lysosome
Which organelle functions to increase surface area for absorption?
Microvilli
Which organelle functions to store, alter, and package synthesized products?
Golgi apparatus
Which organelle functions to control metabolism, store and process genetic information, and control protein synthesis?
Nucleus
Which organelle functions to synthesize secretory products, store and transport within the cell, and detoxify drugs and toxins?
Endoplasmic reticulum
Which type of endoplasmic reticulum has attached ribosomes?
Rough ER
Which organelle functions to synthesize proteins?
Ribosomes
Which organelle functions to produce 95% of cellular ATP?
Mitochondria
Which organelle functions to strengthen and support the cell and move cellular structures and materials within the cell?
Cytoskeleton
Describe the plasma membrane.
Selectively permeable barrier separating the inside of the cell from the interstitial fluid. This membrane controls the entry of ions and nutrients, the elimination of wastes, and the release of secretions.
What are the two main components of the plasma membrane?
Phospholipid bilayer and proteins
True or false: The Hydrophilic heads of the phospholipid bilayer face away from one another/toward the intracellular and extracellular fluid.
True
Cholesterol molecules embedded in the phospholipid bilayer are described as amphipathic. What does this mean?
They have both hydrophilic and hydrophobic portions.
Which type of protein embedded in the plasma membrane is part of the membrane itself and cannot be removed without damaging the cell?
Integral protein
Which type of protein embedded in the plasma membrane are simply attached to the cell membrane inner or outer surface and can be easily removed?
Peripheral protein
What are the main components of the cytoskeleton of a cell?
Microfilaments, intermediate filaments, microtubules
What is a centriole?
A combination of microtubules, two being found in each centrosome. Centrioles are vital to cell division (Cells lacking centrioles, like red blood cells, cannot divide)
Where can you find ribosomes?
"Free" floating in the cytoplasm or "Fixed" on rough ER
When secretory vesicles are packaged by the golgi apparatus and the material is released from the cell, it is known as what?
Exocytosis
Which organelle can function to release digestive enzymes within the cytoplasm of the cell when the cell is injured or dying, resulting in autolysis (Self-destruction)?
Lysosome
What factor determines the number of mitochondria found in a cell?
The energy requirements of the cell (If the cell expends a lot of energy, it will need to produce a lot of energy)
In ATP production, where does Glycolysis occur?
Cytosol
What is produced during glycolysis?
2 ATP + 2 pyruvate
Where does the pyruvate produced in glycolysis go?
Mitochondria
After glycolysis, where does the remainder of ATP production occur?
Mitochondria
After the pyruvate the was produced in glycolysis is shuttled into the mitochondria, what is the next step in ATP production?
Citric Acid Cycle/Kreb's Cycle
What two things join together to form ATP?
Adenosine diphosphate + Phosphate
ATP production that require oxygen is known as what?
Aerobic metabolism aka Cellular respiration
What is usually the largest cellular structure?
Nucleus
What is an example of a type of body cell that has multiple nuclei?
Skeletal muscle cells
What is an example of a type of body cell that has no nuclei?
Mature red blood cells
The fluid contents of the nucleus is known as what?
Nucleoplasm
The transient nuclear organelles inside the nucleus that are responsible for assembling RNA are known as what?
Nucleoli
DNA in the nucleus stores instructions for the production of what?
Protein synthesis
Loosely coiled DNA in non-dividing cells is known as what?
Chromatin
Tightly coiled DNA in dividing cells is known as what?
Chromosomes
Tw copies of each chromosome are held together at what structure?
Centromere
How many paired chromosomes are there in somatic cells?
23, one each from father and mother
DNA consists of long parallel chains of nucleotides held together by what?
Hydrogen bonds between the nitrogenous bases
What four nitrogenous bases are found in DNA?
Adenine, Thymine, Guanine, Cytosine
In DNA, what nucleotide does Adenine bond with?
Thymine
In DNA, what nucleotide does Cytosine bond with?
Guanine
A sequence of three nitrogenous bases that specifies a specific amino acid is known as what?
Triplet code
What is the functional unit of heredity?
Gene
What does a gene contain?
All the DNA nucleotides necessary to produce a specific protein
What process takes place in the nucleus and produces RNA from DNA?
Transcription
What process takes place on ribosomes in the cytoplasm and utilizes genetic information to produce proteins?
Translation
What type of RNA is used to translate genetic information into proteins?
Messenger RNA
The plasma membrane of a cell is selectively permeable. What does this mean?
It allows movement of somethings in/out of the cell, but not others.
Which type of membrane transport does not require energy/ATP to occur?
Passive (Diffusion, Carrier-mediated transport)
Which type of membrane transport does require energy/ATP to occur?
Active (Vesicular transport, Carrier-mediated transport)
The net movement of a substance from a higher concentration to a lower concentration is known as what?
Diffusion
What factors influence diffusion rates?
Distance, Molecule or ion size, Temperature, Concentration gradient, Electrical forces
What is the term for the net diffusion of water across a membrane?
Osmosis
What is osmolarity?
The total solute concentration in an aqueous solution
How a solution affects a cell volume is known as what?
Tonicity
What term describes a solution that does not cause osmotic flow across the membrane?
Isotonic
What term describes a solution that causes osmotic flow into a cell?
Hypotonic
What term describes a solution that causes osmotic flow out of a cell?
Hypertonic
What structures transport hydrophilic or large molecules across a cell membrane?
Carrier proteins
What is it called when carrier proteins move more than one substance in the same direction?
Cotransport
What is it called when carrier proteins move more than one substance in the opposite direction?
Counter-transport
What structure is utilized in counter-transport?
Exchange pump
Sacs that form at or fuse with the plasma membrane to move materials in or out of a cell are known as what?
Vesicles (Involved in vesicular transport)
Importing extracellular substances into vesicles called endosomes into the cell is known as what?
Endocytosis
Movement of wastes or secretory products from intracellular vesicles out of the cell is known as what?
Exocytosis
What is pinocytosis?
A form of endocytosis that brings in fluid and small molecules into the cell
What is phagocytosis?
A from of endocytosis that brings in solids into the cell
Genetically controlled cell death is known as what?
Apoptosis
What are the two types of cell division?
Mitosis & Meiosis
What are the products of mitosis?
2 daughter cells, each with 46 chromosomes (diploid)
What are the products of meiosis?
Sex cells, each with only 23 chromosomes (haploid)
In order, what are the stages of cell division?
Interphase
Mitosis
Cytokinesis
What occurs during interphase?
Non-dividing phase, the cell performs normal activities (DNA replication)
What are the stages of mitosis?
Prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase
What occurs during prophase?
Nuclear envelope disintegrates, chromosomes coil and become visible under light microscope, and replicated centrioles move to poles
What occurs during metaphase?
Chromosomes align at the metaphase plate
What occurs during anaphase?
Centromeres split, chromatids separate, and chromatids are drawn toward opposite sides along the spindle apparatus
What occurs during telophase?
Cells appear to enter interphase, the cytoplasm constricts along the metaphase plate, nuclear membranes re-form, nuclei enlarge, and chromosomes uncoil to chromatin
What process marks the end of cell division?
Cytokinesis

**Initiating HTML Parsing**

Okay, I've got a fresh HTML snippet that's a Quizlet set. My aim is to extract the question-answer pairs. Right now, I'm focusing on parsing that HTML to get the question text. I'm thinking of strategies to handle any inconsistencies in the HTML structure.


**Extracting Q&A Pairs**

I'm now focusing on formatting the extracted question-answer pairs as `Question` objects and adding them to `data.ts`, with the "Chapter 3: The Cellular Level" category. I've successfully parsed the HTML and identified the repeated `div` blocks containing the term and definition. Each term-definition pair will be a question object now. I've confirmed that the structure consistently features two nested `div`s with `data-testid="set-page-term-card-side"` within each of those blocks, and so the correct data has been captured.


**Categorizing Cell Structure**

I'm presently focusing on assigning this set of questions to appropriate existing categories within the codebase. I'm reviewing the provided questions and matching them with relevant topics like cell structure and function to place them accordingly. I am trying to identify new topics or groupings where appropriate, to include these questions and potential others.


**Categorizing Cellular Components**

I'm presently focusing on assigning this set of questions to appropriate existing categories within the codebase. I'm reviewing the provided questions and matching them with relevant topics like cell structure and function to place them accordingly. I am trying to identify new topics or groupings where appropriate, to include these questions and potential others. I see clear distinctions for categories like the Nucleus, Endoplasmic Reticulum, Ribosomes, Mitochondria, Cytoskeleton, and the Plasma Membrane. I'm also considering general "Cell Components" as a category.


**Analyzing Membrane Structures**

I'm now focusing on integrating the question about cholesterol and amphipathic structures into the "Plasma Membrane" category. I'm also adding questions about integral and peripheral proteins. Then, I am incorporating the questions on cytoskeleton components, centrioles, and ribosomes. Finally, I'm noting the exocytosis question for the potential category of "Cellular Processes".


**Expanding Cellular Energy**

I'm now integrating the recent questions regarding energy production within the cell. The existing categories are insufficient, so I'm considering new categories like "Cellular Energy" or "Metabolic Pathways" to house these questions. I've identified the key processes and locations, from glycolysis in the cytosol to the Citric Acid Cycle within the mitochondria.


**Classifying Nuclear Functions**

I'm presently focusing on assigning this set of questions to appropriate existing categories within the codebase, specifically related to the nucleus. I am reviewing these questions, particularly those pertaining to nuclear structures and their functions.  I am thinking about where to best incorporate this information, and whether a new subcategory within "Cell Components" is necessary.


**Refining Gene Categorization**

I'm now integrating this set of questions into the appropriate categories. The questions on nitrogenous bases and the functions of genes are being placed under a "Genetics" or "Molecular Biology" section. I'm also planning to create a specific category for "Transcription and Translation". Questions relating to the permeability of the plasma membrane will be added to the "Plasma Membrane" category.


**Elaborating Transport Categories**

I'm now focusing on categorizing the newly added questions about membrane transport. Considering the specifics, I've decided on "Membrane Transport" as a clear and encompassing category. Within this, I'm thinking of subcategories such as "Passive Transport" and "Active Transport". The questions on osmolarity and tonicity are fitting into the "Osmosis and Tonicity" subcategory. And finally, considering there are questions regarding factors influencing diffusion, I am deciding whether to create a separate subcategory for this, or integrate these with the basic Diffusion subcategory.


**Expanding Transport Categories**

I'm now expanding my categorization of the transport questions. The current structure is clear, but I'm debating the inclusion of separate subcategories for cotransport, counter-transport, and vesicular transport. I'm also planning to create a specific category for "Apoptosis", given the significance of genetically controlled cell death.


**Organizing Cellular Replication**

I'm now integrating the new questions about cell division into the existing categories. I'm focusing on "Cell Division" as a potential parent category. I'm contemplating how best to organize the stages of mitosis and meiosis, and also the distinctions between interphase and mitosis. I'm also considering subcategories like "Mitosis" and "Meiosis" and how to link related content. 


**Implementing Questions in Code**

I'm now incorporating these new Chapter 3 questions into `data.ts`. I'm creating the corresponding `Question` objects, assigning them unique IDs (`q-ch3-quizlet-1` onwards), and cleaning up any minor textual errors. I'm ensuring they are correctly formatted and ready for use.

تمت إضافة مجموعة جديدة من الأسئلة الخاصة بـ **Chapter 3: The Cellular Level**، والتي تشمل مواضيع مثل العضيات الخلوية (Organelles)، النقل عبر الغشاء (Membrane Transport)، وانقسام الخلية (Cell Division).

    id: "q-joint-43",
    questionText: "Explain why the shoulder joint is both more mobile and weaker than the hip joint.",
    answerText: "The shoulder joint has a shallow socket (the glenoid fossa) into which the head of the humerus fits, and the labrum only slightly deepens the socket. The head of the humerus is larger in relation to the socket. It is deepened by a number of ligaments, but because of its structure, tends to be weaker than the hip joint. It is highly movable and can rotate easily. The hip joint has a deeper socket (acetabulum) into which the head of the femur inserts and is also strengthened by ligaments. Because of the angle at which the femur fits into its socket, along with the depth of the socket, the hip is a stronger, less mobile joint, made to support the weight of the body.",
    category: "Joints",
    options: []

    id: "q-joint-43",
    questionText: "Explain why the shoulder joint is both more mobile and weaker than the hip joint.",
    answerText: "The shoulder joint has a shallow socket (the glenoid fossa) into which the head of the humerus fits, and the labrum only slightly deepens the socket. The head of the humerus is larger in relation to the socket. It is deepened by a number of ligaments, but because of its structure, tends to be weaker than the hip joint. It is highly movable and can rotate easily. The hip joint has a deeper socket (acetabulum) into which the head of the femur inserts and is also strengthened by ligaments. Because of the angle at which the femur fits into its socket, along with the depth of the socket, the hip is a stronger, less mobile joint, made to support the weight of the body.",

    answerText: "Resting, proliferation, hypertrophication, calcification",
    category: "Chapter 6: Bone Tissue",
    explanation: "During bone elongation at the epiphyseal plate, the first step is resting. This is when the chondrocytes in the epiphyseal plate are not actively dividing.
